name: scene-transition-whip
description: Whip-pan transition between two scenes. Creates Scene A comp, Scene B comp, and a Main comp that nests both. An adjustment layer spanning the cut point (5 frames before to 5 frames after at the midpoint) applies a Transform effect for shutter blur (360° angle), Fast Box Blur ramping 0→200→0, and a position whip 0→-1880→-2500. Motion Tile extends edges at 400% width. All keyframes use easeInOut. Use this for energetic scene cuts in vlogs, social content, and highlight reels.

scene_a_bg_color.description: Scene A background color [r,g,b] 0-1 — default deep navy #111141

scene_b_bg_color.description: Scene B background color [r,g,b] 0-1 — default deep crimson #41111A

cut_time.description: Time in seconds where the whip cut happens (midpoint of main comp)

transition_frames.description: Total transition duration in frames (5 before + 5 after cut point)

steps.note: Scene A: 4s pre-comp (longer than needed so it has content before the cut point at 3s in Main). Deep navy #111141 background — from the Corporate Dark Stack but with a blue-purple cast. Store returned ID as $sceneAId.
Explicit solid for Scene A background. Solidifies the comp's background color as a renderable layer.

96pt Black centered in Scene A. Off-white #F5F5F5 text on the dark navy. All-caps tracking +150 per mandatory rule. Centered at [960, 540] — frame center (960=1920/2, 540=1080/2) — commanding presence for a simple scene identifier.
Simple 0.3s fade-in on Scene A title at the start of the comp. Establishes the scene without drama — the whip transition provides the energy.

Scene B: 4s pre-comp with deep crimson #41111A background — warm complement to Scene A's cool navy. The contrast between scenes (cool→warm) makes the transition visually meaningful and context-clear. Store returned ID as $sceneBId.
Explicit solid for Scene B background.
Matching structure to Scene A: 96pt Black centered, same font and tracking. Compositional consistency between scenes so the transition itself — not the content difference — is what reads as the cut.
Same 0.3s fade-in on Scene B title. After the whip transition, the viewer needs a moment for the new scene to register — a subtle fade-in helps this read as intentional.
Main comp: 6s total, cut point at t=3.0s. Scene A occupies 0–3s, Scene B occupies 3–6s. Near-black background behind the scenes (visible if edges clip during whip). Store returned ID as $mainCompId.

Nest Scene A comp into Main comp. Scene A positioned at center [960, 540]. This is the pre-comp layer for Scene A — it will be trimmed to 0–3.17s (to include the transition overlap). Store returned layerIndex as $sceneALayerIdx.
Scene A ends at 3.167s (3.0s cut + 5 frames at 29.97fps = 3.0 + 0.167 = 3.167s). The extra 5 frames past the cut point ensure Scene A content is visible DURING the transition's exit phase.
Nest Scene B comp into Main comp. Starts at the pre-cut point (2.833s) to be present during the transition entry, then runs to 6.0s. Store returned layerIndex as $sceneBLayerIdx.

Scene B starts at 2.833s (3.0s cut - 5 frames at 29.97fps = 3.0 - 0.167 = 2.833s). Overlap between Scene A (ends 3.167s) and Scene B (starts 2.833s) creates a 0.334s window where both are present — the adjustment layer handles this transition zone.

Adjustment layer carries all transition effects. This is the correct approach — effects on an adjustment layer apply to all layers beneath it, so both Scene A and Scene B get the same blur/motion treatment simultaneously. Store returned layerIndex as $adjLayerIdx.

Adjustment layer spans ONLY the transition zone: 5 frames before cut (2.833s) to 5 frames after cut (3.167s). Total duration: 10 frames (0.334s at 29.97fps). This precise trimming prevents any effects from leaking into normal playback outside the transition.

Fast Box Blur on the adjustment layer creates the directional motion blur of the whip. Fast Box Blur (not Gaussian Blur) is preferred for whip transitions because it creates harder, more rectangular blur that reads as extreme lateral motion — not just soft defocus.

Blur Radius 0→200→0 across the transition. Peak blur of 200px at the exact cut point (t=3.0s) creates maximum visual obscuration — hides the cut entirely. Symmetric arc: ramps up over 5 frames, peaks at cut, ramps down over 5 frames. 200px is aggressive enough to fully obscure content for a believable whip.
easeInOut on blur ramp — the blur builds and decays smoothly, avoiding any abrupt jump from 0 to full blur. Even 5-frame keyframes benefit from easing for perceptual smoothness.

Repeat Edge Pixels prevents the black-edge artifact that appears when blur extends beyond frame boundaries. This is critical for the adjustment layer approach — without it, dark borders appear at the edges during peak blur.

Motion Tile extends the effective canvas width so that position-offset animation doesn't reveal the black comp background behind the scenes. Output Width 400% means the scene content tiles to fill 4× the frame width — essential when position keyframes move the composition off-screen.

400% Output Width — matches the spec. At 400%, the tiled frame extends 2× the frame width in each direction (left and right), accommodating position moves up to -1920px or +1920px without showing empty canvas.
Mirror Edges on Motion Tile creates a mirrored reflection at tile boundaries rather than repeating — this looks far more natural and film-like during the whip pan than a hard tile repeat.

Transform effect (not the layer Transform property) for the whip position animation. Critical distinction: using the Transform EFFECT allows motion blur at a per-effect level with Shutter Angle, independently from the layer's own motion blur. This is the professional technique for whip transitions.

Shutter Angle 360° on the Transform effect — maximum possible shutter angle creates maximum motion blur for the whip pan movement. At 360°, every frame blurs across its entire duration (vs. cinematic 180°). This is the standard spec for whip pan transitions: exaggerated blur hides the cut.

Position keyframes for the whip pan: Start at center [960,540] → move left to [80,540] (−880px from center) at cut point → continue to [−580,540] (total −1540px from center) at transition end. Y stays constant at 540 — pure lateral whip. The progressive offset (-880 then further -660) mimics the deceleration of a real camera whip: fast initial pan, slight continuation. Note: values here move the effect's anchor; actual displacement relative to frame differs from off-screen positions depending on Motion Tile expansion.

easeInOut on position — builds speed into the cut, decelerates out of it. Even though this is a fast 10-frame animation, easeInOut prevents robotic linear motion and adds the slight inertia-feel of a real camera whip.

NULL layer as a transition controller. Unused in this base example but included as a structural anchor — in production, markers can be placed here to denote the cut point, and expressions on the adjustment layer can reference this null's time markers for dynamic repositioning of the transition.

params.comment: WHIP CUT POINT — adjust transition_frames around this time

steps.note: Marker on the NULL at the exact cut point (t=3.0s). Markers serve as production documentation — editors can scrub to this point and immediately know this is the intended transition hit. Also useful for audio sync.
Label color 6 (Cyan/Teal in AE's label palette) on the adjustment layer — distinguishes it visually in the timeline as a 'technical' or 'effect' layer. Professional timeline organization practice.
Label color 2 (Yellow) on Scene A layer — warm color for the first scene, easy to distinguish in the timeline.
Label color 4 (Pink/Red) on Scene B layer — warm but different from Scene A's yellow for quick visual parsing in the timeline.

Enable motion blur on the adjustment layer itself — this stacks with the Transform effect's Shutter Angle for compound blur. Both the layer motion blur AND the Transform effect blur contribute to the whip effect quality.

Comp-level Shutter Angle also set to 360° for the Main comp — ensures all layers in the main comp receive maximum motion blur during render. 32 samples per frame for the whip comp specifically: the short 10-frame transition window requires high sample count to render smooth, not choppy, blur during the fast movement.
Save after constructing the three-comp structure. The project now contains Scene A, Scene B, and Whip Pan Main — all ready for footage/content replacement by swapping the scene pre-comp contents.
